content.assessment: From a public finance lens, the status-quo counterfactual leaves resource allocation governed by ad hoc administrative discretion and unequal local social capital rather than transparent fiscal criteria. It perpetuates the structural mismatch between low national per-pupil funding and the higher per-pupil overhead of small rural schools, without ever formally weighing consolidation savings against transport and equity costs. Because outcomes depend on informal mobilization rather than rule-based cost accounting, fiscal incidence becomes arbitrary: villages with similar demographics and cost profiles may face divergent outcomes purely due to differential lobbying capacity, which is inefficient from a value-for-money standpoint and creates unpredictable budget exposure for school district centers.
opportunities: A do-nothing baseline could still be paired with incremental, low-cost fiscal transparency measures without requiring full statutory reform.
strengths: Avoids the transition/adjustment costs associated with any new statutory overhaul, since governance oscillation itself is costly (F-live-education_finance-10).
Preserves whatever local funding flexibility currently exists without imposing new compliance costs on district centers.
threats: Continued informal reversal of closure decisions after protest creates fiscal unpredictability for district budget planning, since decisions are not codified (F-live-education_finance-10 analog governance instability logic).
Absent standardized cost accounting, the true comparative cost of retention versus consolidation remains unknown, risking poor value-for-money outcomes system-wide.
weaknesses: No rule-based cost-incidence framework; survival is determined by social capital rather than fiscal criteria, producing inequitable resource distribution across similarly-situated villages (F-live-education_finance-01).

Structural per-pupil funding pressure from national formulas persists unaddressed, and Hungary's comparatively low per-pupil spending ($7,115, rank 25/29) leaves little fiscal slack to informally subsidize small schools that survive by discretion rather than design (F-live-education_finance-07).
Transportation cost offsets and other hidden costs of small/dispersed schools remain uncounted in ad hoc decisions, understating true fiscal exposure (F-live-education_finance-03).
